items.mistake: Computing groupby statistics on the full DataFrame, then merging back, then calling train_test_split
items.fix: Split first (or iterate CV folds); compute groupby aggregates on training rows only; map or merge onto train and test without refitting on test.
items.why: Test rows influence group means/counts assigned to themselves—classic join/preprocessing leakage.
items.example: `df['u_mean'] = df.groupby('user_id')['x'].transform('mean')` on all rows before split → test users leak into their own feature.

items.mistake: Using inner merge when unmatched left rows must be kept for modeling
items.fix: Use how='left' on the fact table side, then inspect NaN rate and impute or flag missing dimension joins explicitly.
items.why: Inner join drops events whose keys are missing in the dimension table, biasing the sample toward well-cataloged entities.
items.example: 10M clicks inner-joined to products drops 2M clicks with stale product_id—model never sees long-tail traffic.

items.mistake: Joining on a non-unique key without checking row-count explosion
items.fix: Assert len(merged) == len(left) when expecting m:1; use merge(..., validate='m:1') and indicator=True during development.
items.why: Many-to-many joins duplicate rows, inflating sample weight and letting near-duplicates cross train/test boundaries.
items.example: Merge patients to diagnoses on hospital_id instead of patient_id → row count jumps 40×.

items.mistake: Merging label or post-outcome columns into features before partitioning data
items.fix: Keep labels separate until after feature engineering on train; join labels only inside each fold or on the training slice for fit.
items.why: Label columns merged early become indistinguishable from features and encode the target directly.
items.example: Early merge of refunds table with will_refund column into click features before split → near-perfect offline AUC.
